title: Prolactinoma
oneLiner: Benign pituitary adenoma causing hyperprolactinaemia; presents with hypogonadism, galactorrhoea, or mass effect; highly responsive to medical therapy.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Pituitary apoplexy** (haemorrhage/infarction into adenoma): sudden severe headache + vomiting + diplopia/ophthalmoplegia + ↓consciousness + hypotension – urgent MRI + neurosurgery referral + IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat (do not delay steroids for imaging).
- **Bacterial meningitis** (rare if apoplexy causes CSF leak): fever + neck stiffness + photophobia – blood cultures, IV antibiotics, CT head before LP if focal neurology.
sections.Presentation:
- **Women (reproductive age):** oligo/amenorrhoea, galactorrhoea, infertility, loss of libido, dyspareunia (vaginal dryness).
- **Men:** erectile dysfunction, ↓libido, infertility (oligospermia), gynaecomastia (rare).
- **Both sexes:** headache, visual field deficit (typically bitemporal superior quadrantanopia → hemianopia), symptoms of hypopituitarism (fatigue, cold intolerance, hypotension, reduced axillary/pubic hair).
- **Macroprolactinoma (>10 mm):** mass effect – headache, visual loss, cranial nerve palsies (III, IV, VI), + rarely hydrocephalus.
- **Incidental finding** on brain imaging for other reasons.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Drug-induced hyperprolactinaemia** (antipsychotics, metoclopramide, domperidone, SSRIs, verapamil, methyldopa) – check drug history + dose correlation.
- **Hypothyroidism** (TRH stimulates prolactin) – check TSH/free T4; hyperprolactinaemia resolves with levothyroxine.
- **Renal failure** (decreased prolactin clearance) – check urea/creatinine.
- **Pregnancy / lactation** – pregnancy test + history.
- **Non-functioning pituitary adenoma with stalk compression** (disinhibits prolactin – “stalk effect”) – prolactin usually <5000 mIU/L, often macroadenoma with hypopituitarism.
- **Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS)** – hyperandrogenism, LH/FSH ratio >2, normal prolactin (but can be mildly elevated).
- **Physiological stress** (seizures, chest wall trauma, nipple stimulation) – transient, resolves.

**Splitter:** Prolactin >5000 mIU/L (or >200 µg/L) is almost diagnostic of prolactinoma; levels 2000-5000 may be stalk effect – MRI differentiates.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** urine dip (pregnancy test) – mandatory in women of childbearing age; visual field testing by confrontation (or formal perimetry if abnormal).
- **Bloods:** serum prolactin (serial – avoid stress, single level >2000 mIU/L suggests adenoma), TSH, free T4, urea/creatinine, pregnancy test (urine or serum β-hCG), LH/FSH, oestradiol (women) or testosterone (men), cortisol (9 am if hypopituitarism suspected), baseline pituitary function.
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First-line:** MRI pituitary with contrast (thin slices through sella) – identifies adenoma, size, invasion, apoplexy.  
  - **Second-line:** CT head if MRI contraindicated – less sensitive for small lesions.
- **Special tests:**  
  - **Visual field perimetry** (Goldmann or automated) – all patients with adenoma touching optic chiasm; repeat post-treatment.  
  - **Pituitary function tests** (if suspected hypopituitarism): 9 am cortisol, synacthen test, IGF-1 for GH axis.
- **Diagnostic criteria:**  
  - Prolactin >2000 mIU/L (or >100 µg/L) with no other cause → presumptive prolactinoma.  
  - MRI confirms adenoma; size <10 mm = microprolactinoma, ≥10 mm = macroprolactinoma.
- **Never-miss test:** **Pregnancy test** in any woman with amenorrhoea/galactorrhoea; **visual fields** in any patient with a pituitary mass.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - If suspected apoplexy: IV hydrocortisone 100 mg + stat neurosurgery referral + urgent MRI pituitary.  
  - If significant visual loss or neurological deficit: same day neurosurgery referral.  
  - Measure prolactin (if not already) – do not wait for result before treatment.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Start dopamine agonist (cabergoline first-line) – see Drugs & Doses.  
  - Check baseline prolactin, then repeat weekly until normal.  
  - If macroprolactinoma: visual field testing pre-treatment and at 1 week (risk of chiasmal herniation – rare but important).  
  - Assess for hypopituitarism: 9 am cortisol, TFTs – replace hydrocortisone and/or levothyroxine if deficient.  
  - Monitor for side effects: nausea, postural hypotension, mood changes, impulse control disorders.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Continue cabergoline; aim for prolactin normalisation and tumour shrinkage.  
  - Titrate dose monthly based on prolactin levels (usually 0.5 mg twice weekly → up to 2 mg twice weekly).  
  - Repeat MRI at 1 year (sooner if visual worsening or no response).  
  - Surgery (transsphenoidal adenectomy) if: drug intolerance, resistance to dopamine agonists, apoplexy, or optic chiasm compression despite medical therapy.  
  - Radiotherapy for residual/recurrent aggressive tumours (rarely needed).  
  - Monitor for cardiovascular effects of long-term cabergoline: consider echocardiogram if high cumulative dose or symptoms of valvulopathy.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: 0.9% saline at maintenance rate if euvolemic.  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (adjusted for weight/renal function).  
  - Diet: nil-by-mouth if awaiting surgery; regular diet otherwise.  
  - Observations: standard NEWS2 (4-hourly).  
  - If apoplexy: 1-hourly GCS + neuro observations in HDU.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line:** **Cabergoline** – 0.5 mg orally twice weekly (e.g. Monday and Thursday). Titrate by 0.5 mg per month up to 4 mg weekly (max 4.5 mg/week). Duration: indefinite (often lifelong).  
- **Alternative:** **Bromocriptine** – 1.25 mg/nocte initially, increase slowly to 2.5 mg twice or three times daily (max 30 mg/day). More side effects (nausea, postural hypotension) – rarely used first-line.  
- **Key contraindications:** Pregnancy (relative – cabergoline is usually stopped once pregnancy confirmed; bromocriptine used in women wanting conception); uncontrolled hypertension (with bromocriptine – risk of stroke).  
- **Interactions:** Metoclopramide/domperidone (antagonise effect), antipsychotics (dopamine antagonists – reduce efficacy), SSRIs (mild prolactin elevation).  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No dose adjustment needed for mild-moderate renal impairment; severe dysfunction [check local policy]. Hepatic impairment – avoid cabergoline if severe (Child-Pugh C) due to risk of accumulation.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Starting too high a dose of cabergoline** (e.g. 1 mg twice weekly from day 1) → severe postural hypotension, syncope, falls, or psychosis (especially in elderly). Always start low, titrate slowly. In patients with known impulse control disorders, monitor for pathological gambling/hypersexuality.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Pituitary apoplexy – sudden headache, visual loss, ophthalmoplegia, adrenal crisis.  
  - CSF leak (post-surgery or spontaneous with macroadenoma) – clear rhinorrhoea, glucose-positive.  
  - Drug-induced: nausea, postural hypotension, headache, nasal congestion, psychosis (rare), impulse control disorders.
- **Chronic:**  
  - Hypogonadism → infertility, osteoporosis (if untreated).  
  - Visual field defects (irreversible if untreated for >2 weeks).  
  - Hypopituitarism (macroprolactinoma compressing normal pituitary).  
  - Valvular heart disease (very rare with cabergoline at standard doses – cumulative >2 mg weekly over years).  
  - Growth of adenoma during pregnancy (rare – microprolactinomas usually safe, macroprolactinomas require serial visual fields and consideration of bromocriptine).
sections.Prognosis:
- **Excellent** – >90% of patients normalise prolactin and achieve tumour shrinkage with cabergoline.  
- **Surgery curative** in 60-80% of microprolactinomas; lower for macroprolactinomas (often need ongoing medical therapy).  
- **Recurrence** after drug withdrawal: common in macroprolactinomas (80% relapse); microprolactinomas ~25% relapse at 5 years.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check drug history** for dopamine antagonists before diagnosing prolactinoma – this is the most common cause of moderate hyperprolactinaemia.
- **Perform visual fields** (confrontation + formal perimetry) in all patients with macroprolactinoma or visual symptoms – document baseline before starting treatment.
- **Counsel women of childbearing age** about dopamine agonist safety in pregnancy (switch to bromocriptine if planning pregnancy; stop once pregnancy confirmed and monitor with visual fields).
- **Check 9 am cortisol** before starting cabergoline – if low, give hydrocortisone first (stress of starting treatment can unmask adrenal insufficiency).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not treat** mildly elevated prolactin (<2000 mIU/L) without checking TSH, renal function, and drug history – could be hypothyroidism or stalk effect.
- **Do not stop dopamine agonists** abruptly after symptom resolution – most patients need lifelong therapy; sudden withdrawal can cause tumour regrowth and apoplexy.
- **Do not assume** that a normal MRI excludes prolactinoma – a microadenoma <5 mm may be missed; if prolactin >2000 mIU/L and MRI negative, consider stalk effect or macroprolactinoma with empty sella.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - New severe headache, vomiting, or visual loss (apoplexy – go to ED immediately).  
  - Signs of meningitis (fever, stiff neck) if suspected CSF leak.  
  - Symptoms of adrenal insufficiency (fatigue, dizziness, hypotension).  
  - New impulse control behaviours (gambling, spending, hypersexuality) – may need dose reduction.
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Endocrinology clinic within 2-4 weeks of starting treatment.  
  - Repeat prolactin in 2 weeks from initial dosing, then monthly until normal.  
  - MRI pituitary at 1 year (or sooner if macroadenoma and optic chiasm involvement – at 3 months post-treatment).  
  - Visual fields at 1 week and 3 months for macroprolactinoma.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (SpR/Registrar) if:**  
  - Suspected pituitary apoplexy: severe headache + vomiting + ocular signs + ↓GCS.  
  - New visual field defect or worsening vision.  
  - Prolactin >10,000 mIU/L (increased apoplexy risk).  
  - Signs of adrenal crisis (hypotension, hyponatraemia, hypoglycaemia) – give hydrocortisone.
- **Call ICU/Anaesthetic team if:**  
  - GCS <13 or rapid deterioration.  
  - Hemodynamic instability unresponsive to fluids.  
  - Suspicion of meningitis with sepsis.
- **Crash team if:**  
  - Cardiac arrest or airway compromise (rare).

rawMarkdown:
## Prolactinoma
**One-liner:** Benign pituitary adenoma causing hyperprolactinaemia; presents with hypogonadism, galactorrhoea, or mass effect; highly responsive to medical therapy.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Pituitary apoplexy** (haemorrhage/infarction into adenoma): sudden severe headache + vomiting + diplopia/ophthalmoplegia + ↓consciousness + hypotension – urgent MRI + neurosurgery referral + IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat (do not delay steroids for imaging).
- **Bacterial meningitis** (rare if apoplexy causes CSF leak): fever + neck stiffness + photophobia – blood cultures, IV antibiotics, CT head before LP if focal neurology.

### Presentation
- **Women (reproductive age):** oligo/amenorrhoea, galactorrhoea, infertility, loss of libido, dyspareunia (vaginal dryness).
- **Men:** erectile dysfunction, ↓libido, infertility (oligospermia), gynaecomastia (rare).
- **Both sexes:** headache, visual field deficit (typically bitemporal superior quadrantanopia → hemianopia), symptoms of hypopituitarism (fatigue, cold intolerance, hypotension, reduced axillary/pubic hair).
- **Macroprolactinoma (>10 mm):** mass effect – headache, visual loss, cranial nerve palsies (III, IV, VI), + rarely hydrocephalus.
- **Incidental finding** on brain imaging for other reasons.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Drug-induced hyperprolactinaemia** (antipsychotics, metoclopramide, domperidone, SSRIs, verapamil, methyldopa) – check drug history + dose correlation.
- **Hypothyroidism** (TRH stimulates prolactin) – check TSH/free T4; hyperprolactinaemia resolves with levothyroxine.
- **Renal failure** (decreased prolactin clearance) – check urea/creatinine.
- **Pregnancy / lactation** – pregnancy test + history.
- **Non-functioning pituitary adenoma with stalk compression** (disinhibits prolactin – “stalk effect”) – prolactin usually <5000 mIU/L, often macroadenoma with hypopituitarism.
- **Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS)** – hyperandrogenism, LH/FSH ratio >2, normal prolactin (but can be mildly elevated).
- **Physiological stress** (seizures, chest wall trauma, nipple stimulation) – transient, resolves.

**Splitter:** Prolactin >5000 mIU/L (or >200 µg/L) is almost diagnostic of prolactinoma; levels 2000-5000 may be stalk effect – MRI differentiates.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** urine dip (pregnancy test) – mandatory in women of childbearing age; visual field testing by confrontation (or formal perimetry if abnormal).
- **Bloods:** serum prolactin (serial – avoid stress, single level >2000 mIU/L suggests adenoma), TSH, free T4, urea/creatinine, pregnancy test (urine or serum β-hCG), LH/FSH, oestradiol (women) or testosterone (men), cortisol (9 am if hypopituitarism suspected), baseline pituitary function.
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First-line:** MRI pituitary with contrast (thin slices through sella) – identifies adenoma, size, invasion, apoplexy.  
  - **Second-line:** CT head if MRI contraindicated – less sensitive for small lesions.
- **Special tests:**  
  - **Visual field perimetry** (Goldmann or automated) – all patients with adenoma touching optic chiasm; repeat post-treatment.  
  - **Pituitary function tests** (if suspected hypopituitarism): 9 am cortisol, synacthen test, IGF-1 for GH axis.
- **Diagnostic criteria:**  
  - Prolactin >2000 mIU/L (or >100 µg/L) with no other cause → presumptive prolactinoma.  
  - MRI confirms adenoma; size <10 mm = microprolactinoma, ≥10 mm = macroprolactinoma.
- **Never-miss test:** **Pregnancy test** in any woman with amenorrhoea/galactorrhoea; **visual fields** in any patient with a pituitary mass.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - If suspected apoplexy: IV hydrocortisone 100 mg + stat neurosurgery referral + urgent MRI pituitary.  
  - If significant visual loss or neurological deficit: same day neurosurgery referral.  
  - Measure prolactin (if not already) – do not wait for result before treatment.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Start dopamine agonist (cabergoline first-line) – see Drugs & Doses.  
  - Check baseline prolactin, then repeat weekly until normal.  
  - If macroprolactinoma: visual field testing pre-treatment and at 1 week (risk of chiasmal herniation – rare but important).  
  - Assess for hypopituitarism: 9 am cortisol, TFTs – replace hydrocortisone and/or levothyroxine if deficient.  
  - Monitor for side effects: nausea, postural hypotension, mood changes, impulse control disorders.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Continue cabergoline; aim for prolactin normalisation and tumour shrinkage.  
  - Titrate dose monthly based on prolactin levels (usually 0.5 mg twice weekly → up to 2 mg twice weekly).  
  - Repeat MRI at 1 year (sooner if visual worsening or no response).  
  - Surgery (transsphenoidal adenectomy) if: drug intolerance, resistance to dopamine agonists, apoplexy, or optic chiasm compression despite medical therapy.  
  - Radiotherapy for residual/recurrent aggressive tumours (rarely needed).  
  - Monitor for cardiovascular effects of long-term cabergoline: consider echocardiogram if high cumulative dose or symptoms of valvulopathy.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: 0.9% saline at maintenance rate if euvolemic.  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (adjusted for weight/renal function).  
  - Diet: nil-by-mouth if awaiting surgery; regular diet otherwise.  
  - Observations: standard NEWS2 (4-hourly).  
  - If apoplexy: 1-hourly GCS + neuro observations in HDU.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line:** **Cabergoline** – 0.5 mg orally twice weekly (e.g. Monday and Thursday). Titrate by 0.5 mg per month up to 4 mg weekly (max 4.5 mg/week). Duration: indefinite (often lifelong).  
- **Alternative:** **Bromocriptine** – 1.25 mg/nocte initially, increase slowly to 2.5 mg twice or three times daily (max 30 mg/day). More side effects (nausea, postural hypotension) – rarely used first-line.  
- **Key contraindications:** Pregnancy (relative – cabergoline is usually stopped once pregnancy confirmed; bromocriptine used in women wanting conception); uncontrolled hypertension (with bromocriptine – risk of stroke).  
- **Interactions:** Metoclopramide/domperidone (antagonise effect), antipsychotics (dopamine antagonists – reduce efficacy), SSRIs (mild prolactin elevation).  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No dose adjustment needed for mild-moderate renal impairment; severe dysfunction [check local policy]. Hepatic impairment – avoid cabergoline if severe (Child-Pugh C) due to risk of accumulation.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Starting too high a dose of cabergoline** (e.g. 1 mg twice weekly from day 1) → severe postural hypotension, syncope, falls, or psychosis (especially in elderly). Always start low, titrate slowly. In patients with known impulse control disorders, monitor for pathological gambling/hypersexuality.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Pituitary apoplexy – sudden headache, visual loss, ophthalmoplegia, adrenal crisis.  
  - CSF leak (post-surgery or spontaneous with macroadenoma) – clear rhinorrhoea, glucose-positive.  
  - Drug-induced: nausea, postural hypotension, headache, nasal congestion, psychosis (rare), impulse control disorders.
- **Chronic:**  
  - Hypogonadism → infertility, osteoporosis (if untreated).  
  - Visual field defects (irreversible if untreated for >2 weeks).  
  - Hypopituitarism (macroprolactinoma compressing normal pituitary).  
  - Valvular heart disease (very rare with cabergoline at standard doses – cumulative >2 mg weekly over years).  
  - Growth of adenoma during pregnancy (rare – microprolactinomas usually safe, macroprolactinomas require serial visual fields and consideration of bromocriptine).

### Prognosis
- **Excellent** – >90% of patients normalise prolactin and achieve tumour shrinkage with cabergoline.  
- **Surgery curative** in 60-80% of microprolactinomas; lower for macroprolactinomas (often need ongoing medical therapy).  
- **Recurrence** after drug withdrawal: common in macroprolactinomas (80% relapse); microprolactinomas ~25% relapse at 5 years.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check drug history** for dopamine antagonists before diagnosing prolactinoma – this is the most common cause of moderate hyperprolactinaemia.
- **Perform visual fields** (confrontation + formal perimetry) in all patients with macroprolactinoma or visual symptoms – document baseline before starting treatment.
- **Counsel women of childbearing age** about dopamine agonist safety in pregnancy (switch to bromocriptine if planning pregnancy; stop once pregnancy confirmed and monitor with visual fields).
- **Check 9 am cortisol** before starting cabergoline – if low, give hydrocortisone first (stress of starting treatment can unmask adrenal insufficiency).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not treat** mildly elevated prolactin (<2000 mIU/L) without checking TSH, renal function, and drug history – could be hypothyroidism or stalk effect.
- **Do not stop dopamine agonists** abruptly after symptom resolution – most patients need lifelong therapy; sudden withdrawal can cause tumour regrowth and apoplexy.
- **Do not assume** that a normal MRI excludes prolactinoma – a microadenoma <5 mm may be missed; if prolactin >2000 mIU/L and MRI negative, consider stalk effect or macroprolactinoma with empty sella.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - New severe headache, vomiting, or visual loss (apoplexy – go to ED immediately).  
  - Signs of meningitis (fever, stiff neck) if suspected CSF leak.  
  - Symptoms of adrenal insufficiency (fatigue, dizziness, hypotension).  
  - New impulse control behaviours (gambling, spending, hypersexuality) – may need dose reduction.
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Endocrinology clinic within 2-4 weeks of starting treatment.  
  - Repeat prolactin in 2 weeks from initial dosing, then monthly until normal.  
  - MRI pituitary at 1 year (or sooner if macroadenoma and optic chiasm involvement – at 3 months post-treatment).  
  - Visual fields at 1 week and 3 months for macroprolactinoma.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (SpR/Registrar) if:**  
  - Suspected pituitary apoplexy: severe headache + vomiting + ocular signs + ↓GCS.  
  - New visual field defect or worsening vision.  
  - Prolactin >10,000 mIU/L (increased apoplexy risk).  
  - Signs of adrenal crisis (hypotension, hyponatraemia, hypoglycaemia) – give hydrocortisone.
- **Call ICU/Anaesthetic team if:**  
  - GCS <13 or rapid deterioration.  
  - Hemodynamic instability unresponsive to fluids.  
  - Suspicion of meningitis with sepsis.
- **Crash team if:**  
  - Cardiac arrest or airway compromise (rare).
